macro_segments.title: The Mishna's Case of Nafal Lifanav: Forward vs. Backward Falls into a Pit

micro_segments.title: Introduction to the Case: Digging in a Pit and Animal Startlement
Rav's Position: Hevel (Air) as the Critical Factor for Liability
Shmuel's Position: Both Air and Impact Create Full Liability
Resolving the Mishna: Forward Fall into the Pit vs. Backward Trip Outside It

macro_segments.title: Defending Rav Against the Baraisa: Special Cases and Distinctions

micro_segments.title: The Baraisa Contradiction: Both Face-First and Backward Falls Obligate the Pit Owner
Rav Chisda's Resolution: Pit in One's Own Property (Bor BiReshuto)
Rava's Explanation: The Animal Flipped After Initial Face-First Fall
Rav Yosef's Alternative: Liability for Damage to Water, Not the Ox
Rabbi Chanina's Support: Natural Manner of Falling (Derech Nefilah)

macro_segments.title: The Role of Stumbling: Does the Pit Need to Cause the Stumble?

micro_segments.title: The Question: Can the Digger's Sound Absolve the Pit Owner of Liability?
Rav Shimi bar Ashi: Rabbi Noson's Position—Pit Owner Liable Despite Not Causing Stumble
Tannaic Source: One Ox Pushes Another into Pit—Ox Owner vs. Pit Owner
Chachamim vs. Rabbi Noson: Stumble-Focused vs. Damage-Focused Liability

macro_segments.title: Liability Division: Tam vs. Mu'ad and Full vs. Half Liability Models

micro_segments.title: The Contradiction: Rabbi Noson Says 50/50 in One Baraisa and 3/4 to 1/4 in Another
Reconciliation: Tam vs. Mu'ad—How Liability Percentages Differ
100% vs. 50/50 Conceptual Models: Rava's Explanation of Rabbi Noson's Framework
The Ox Says: Why Should I Still Pay Half When Both Are Liable?
Alternative Explanation: Full Liability for the Pit, Deductible from Other Parties

macro_segments.title: Stone at Pit's Edge: When the Stumbling Object is Not the Ox Itself

micro_segments.title: Rava's Case: Rock Placed at Pit Entrance Causes Animal to Stumble and Fall
Why Not Obvious: The Stone Has No Independent Utility Without the Pit
The Pit Owner's Argument: My Pit Is Required for Any Damage to Occur
Gemara's Resolution: The Debate Still Applies; Both Factors Were Necessary

macro_segments.title: Multiple Actors and Exempt Categories: Ox, Human, and Pit in Various Scenarios

micro_segments.title: Setup: Two Oxen of Invalid Sacrifices Gore—Abaye vs. Ravina on Payment Amount
Interpretation 1: Tam vs. Mu'ad Distinction Explains the Different Payments
Interpretation 2: Both Follow Rabbanim; Different Liability Percentages by Status
Rava's Comprehensive Example: Ox and Human Pushing Into Pit
Four Payment Categories: Nezek, Tza'ar/Rippui/Shevet/Boshet, Kofer, and Kelim

macro_segments.title: The Exemption for Invalid Sacrifices: What the Verse 'V'Hameit Yihyeh Lo' Teaches

micro_segments.title: Rava's Question: Why Is an Ox of Invalid Sacrifices Exempt When Falling into a Pit?
Two Interpretations of 'V'Hameit Yihyeh Lo': Usable Carcass vs. Total Exemption
Gemara's Reasoning: The Pit Has Other Categorical Exemptions (Kelim), So Add This One
Why Not the Ox? The Ox Never Pays Categorically for Certain Objects
Conclusion: This Framework Allows the Illustrative Cases of Pit, Ox, and Human
